As I am writing this, Justin's average rating on Planet Terp is a 4.69, which is even higher than Christopher Kauffman's average rating (4.63). I had Kauffman for CMSC216 in Spring 2025, and he was one of my favorite professors of all time. Needless to say, given all the hype around Justin, I had high hopes going into his Summer 2025 CMSC351 class. Now that everything's over, I can say in short that I am disappointed with my experience.

To be clear, Algorithms as a Computer Science class is commonly agreed to be a terrible class pretty much everywhere (based on talking to many people from many different universities). A large part of this is due to the fact that those with minimal experience in algorithmic programming find the class extremely difficult, while at the same time those with a lot of experience in algorithmic programming (i.e. competitive programmers, like myself) often dislike how it is taught very mathematically rigorously yet with little intuition or application at all (in other words, this class will not get you through a coding interview). Yes, Algorithms, the Computer Science class, is more of a math class than anything else. Yes, Computer Science is not just programming, and Theoretical Computer Science consists almost entirely of pure math. All of this I knew going in.

None of this prepared me for when I lost over half the points on a problem from Homework 1 asking me to prove the Big Omega of a certain function. I approached it as almost everyone intuitively would; like almost everyone had been taught to solve an inequality since middle and high school. Set up the inequality. Turn it into an equality, and solve the equality. Determine which side of the solution to the equality contains the solutions to the inequality, and turn the solution to the equality back into an inequality. Apparently, this wasn't rigorous enough for this class. Perhaps this is just how this kind of stuff works. But we were never told that solving it this way would lose us points, and the "correct" way shown in lecture seemed unnecessary and unintuitive. Instead, Justin complained that many submitted solutions to such Big-Omega or Big-O proof problems looked the way AI would solve them, and that such proofs "were generally terrible", citing several reasons why. Okay, fine.

If you take Justin, make sure you explain the hell out of any answer you give to a problem. One problem on Exam 1 asked me to find a lower bound for something and to show my work. I later received minimal points for my answer, despite the fact that the work I had shown lined up almost exactly with a potential full-score answer as described in the rubric. Upon submitting a regrade request, I was told the grader had no idea what I did here at all, since I had no explanation at all. But the question never asked me to explain! Too bad.

There was an Exam 1 MCQ problem which asked whether a certain algorithm was O(N^2) in the best case. The algorithm in question was O(N) in the best case and O(N^2) in the worst case. As such, most people in the class answered incorrectly, as technically an algorithm which is O(N) is also O(N^2). A big stink was made on Piazza about this being a trick question hidden in a sea of 50 multiple-choice questions, designed to make students lose points even if they understood the concepts it was supposed to be testing. One student asked, to avoid confusion, why didn't Justin instead ask if the algorithm was O(N^100) in the best case? Another student literally included a screenshot of the Merriam-Webster entry for "trick question" in their post. In the end, Justin's response was that technically the algorithm was indeed O(N^2) in the best case, and that he didn't expect his students to autopilot any questions in his class.

On top of this, there was a question on the Exam 2 MCQ which, depending on your interpretation of the question (the wording was not specific enough), could have had two different answers. However, if you picked the wrong one, you had no recourse, not even through Piazza. By this point, people had long since stopped posting on Piazza about questions like this. They knew nothing would result from their efforts.

On Exam 3, there was a free-response question which essentially asked us to find the shortest path between two points in "a simple, connected graph" (I'm simplifying greatly). Many test takers realized quickly the looseness of this wording and realized that, to cover all graphs which could potentially be described as "a simple connected graph", one needed to run Floyd's Algorithm, as a simple BFS would not cover the case of a weighted graph, and Dijkstra's would not cover the case of negative-weight edges. Once Exam 3 was graded, everyone who wrote such a solution to that problem lost points (there was a special "tier" in the Gradescope "rubric" for Floyd/Dijkstra solutions). I asked in my regrade request what the issue with my answer was. The response was that I needed to specify what my input to Floyd's Algorithm was or what graph I was going to run it on. I responded that in my answer I had specified running Floyd's algorithm on precisely the graph described in the problem statement. The response? "It does not define distances". Confused and a little frustrated, I wandered over to take a look at the official Exam 3 solutions. The official solution to that problem? Run a BFS.

Justin is not the worst professor I've ever had (currently, that (dis)honor goes to Manoussos Grillakis). Justin is not the devil. Justin is not someone who you need to avoid at all costs. But as a student in his Summer 2025 CMSC351 class, I felt like the environment I was in was just a little bit more hostile than I was used to. Every time I made a Piazza post, I was just a little bit nervous that I'd get flamed by Justin in his response. I felt a severe lack of benefit of the doubt, almost to the point of actively trying to take away points from correct or mostly correct answers.

Look. Everyone's different, and everyone is looking for different things in the professors they get. Lord knows I have some unpopular opinions regarding some UMD professors (both positively and negatively). You might enjoy Justin when you take him, or he might even have just been going through something this summer. Even though a large portion of the final grades were exams (50% exams, 33% final), its definitely possible to come out with a good grade in the class with not too much effort.

This was overall a pretty great class and I would highly recommend Justin. The break down was: 
1/6 - 10 Homework Assignments (1 a week apart from exam weeks)
3/6 - 3 Midterm Exams
2/6 - 1 Final Exam

The homework's often had a few problems which were more difficult than what you would see on an exam, but doing them really helped you learn the material and made sure you really understood what was being taught. The exams themselves were very fair. There was never any problem which was a complete curve ball, and if you understood the material, they were very doable. He also posts very detailed hand written lecture notes, so even if you miss class, you can easily just look at the notes. I overall found this class to pretty interesting and I thought Justin did a great job explaining the concepts during lecture.
Living proof that a smart professor and a good professor are two different things. Avoid him at all costs. I repeat. All costs.
This class is very unorganized, exams have averages of 50s, and the final had an average of 46. To make matters worse, grades have still not been submitted and they were due over a week ago.

I think this is the last time Justin is teaching 351 in-semester for a while, but certainly take him when you can. I'll be taking him for 420. We shall see how the 7-exam system works, but 351 is as of writing the more traditional 3 50-minute exams and a final which are 83.33% (500 of 600 points) of the course.

Pros:
- He's done this so many times that he can guide everyone from start to end of the topics and the course without many hiccups. 
- The entire official class notes are already posted, and he just rewrites a section of them for each lecture.
- Exams are straighforward, follow the 3ish practice exams you get. They're always the same difficulty level, no curveballs.
- You'll lose a few points on the homeworks here and there but they're generally helpful for understanding stuff.
- Great guy living a great life. If you need anything, he'll help where he can. 
- Can't emphasize enough how nice it is to not have shenanigans going on in a class. No games to be played, just learn content however you do best, do homeworks, take exams, repeat.

Cons?
- Really the only downside that I could imagine is that he keeps having to simplify the course, so we go slower than we could and don't learn as much as we used to. It's cool stuff, and I wouldn't mind learning more of it.
- I guess I should mention 351 has no discussion (didn't see a need for one), and Justin doesn't do recordings, which again there's daily handwritten notes and the permanent full body of LaTeX notes.

For me, it was worth getting up at 6am to commute and go to all the 8am lectures. Prioritize getting a seat in a Justin section.

As the last review says, this is also my first time writing a review (that says enough already). This is by far the most disorganized, poorly structured, and poorly taught class I have ever taken by far. If you've walked by Kruskal's office in Iribe, that about says enough. Now for some perhaps, a structure where you learn from poorly recorded videos and irrelevant in-person lectures is just a blast, where you don't know midterm and final topics until 5 days before the exams. Comparing this to taking CMSC330 simultaneously, this is a complete 180, where that class has plenty of practice material, notes and lecture recordings available, etc. You have nothing to study for on exams, expect practically needing to memorize all the relevant recorded lectures, as well as having a chance at a problem or two appearing from a previous exam a few years back. Note that all you are provided for study material, is a previous exam, and irrelevant practice questions that exist to get you to think critically, and that's about it. Even then, 1/3 of the questions you will be faced, are "thinking" problems that have little relevance to anything you've learned. Quizzes are due the morning of the in person lecture, and often aren't announced or posted until the evening of the day before, so god forbid you're studying or doing work for another class, you have no choice but to focus on that first.

In terms of the TAs, it's almost as if they tried to find the most fitting ones, in correspondence to what the professor is like. The TAs (like the professor himself) never reply to emails, or if you get lucky, one will reply in a week, asking questions in Piazza is also useless, I really don't even know why this class had one. They took forever to grade things, even simple multiple choice quizzes, not to mention not getting your exam feedback returned until the next exam comes around. As for the professor, he is more likely than not on tenure, concerns have been voiced regarding his teaching millions of times, and nothing has been done. You will not even get a GLANCE at what your grade is in the class, til grades are due (note that your final and other quizzes are still not graded, that's why I said a glance). You will spend your whole semester biting your nails and ripping your hair out, not knowing what the cutoff will be like, how you're doing in the class, and what curveball will be thrown at you next.

If that isn't enough, grades were due on Dec. 23, and get ready for this...ITS THE NEXT YEAR and there is still no grade in Testudo. 

It's important to add, that Kruskal grades on a curve, so when you see the class averaging 50 on exams, I wouldn't think too much of it, as I'll provide the cutoffs below. I don't understand how this could be the best way to go about grading for this class. Why not create manageable problems, curriculum, exams, etc. and grade on a more normal scale, perhaps adjusting cutoffs, like all the other CS classes do? Note that as far as I understand it, as long as you land within the standard deviation on exams, given your quiz and homework grades are good enough, you will pass.

I certainly have a lot more on my mind that I've gathered throughout the semester, but for the reader's purposes I will end it off on some tips and class information.

Tips:
   -Memorize those lecture recordings like there's no tomorrow. This is practically a self-learning course, and this is about 99% of your learning resources.
   -Do the Homeworks yourself! Only once finished check with your friend or ChatGPT. Exams will consist of one or two reworded homework problems.
   -In-person lectures are mostly a waste of time, unless you're genuinely curious about the irrelevant information provided to you, so use that time to study instead.
   -Your best practice material will be a previous year's exam and your homeworks, and those consist of 50 to 66% of the exam
   -For the "thinking" problems, try to get some practice online or from Leetcode, based on what the "thinking" problem is like from the previous exam
   -Only rely on all of the above if you were not able to get a spot in Justin's class, but I would do everything in my ability to go back and take his class instead.

Before I provide grading metrics, I want to summarize that this is a counter-intuitive, self-learning course, with an elderly hard of hearing professor, who doesn't plan on changing or modifying the structure of his course whatsoever. I am by any means not a morning person, but I do consider myself to be organized, so if sacrificing those early hours of sleep and not losing my ability to sleep is available (along with receiving a better education), choose Justin, and do everything in your power to do so. Last but not least, even with the tips provided above, you will still often encounter problems on exams that are nothing like what you studied for, and it's not uncommon to encounter a topic which lacks fundamental resources to even know what to expect from it. Either start meditating or choose a faith, because that's all that can protect your mental state here. https://www.cs.umd.edu/classconcern

   -6 lowest quizzes were dropped

Good luck.

I found his personality very interesting but dear god where his exams the worst exams I have ever taken. 85% was dictated on 1-2 midterms and a final which aimed to ask a handful of complex problems instead of a lot of smaller ones. I took him for 351 which is all about algorithms and efficiency but this meant that if you just didn't have a good idea for an algorithm to tackle the question he asks then that is 20 points off the exam. There is a shaky relationship with partial credit since the TAs are grading off of his pretty strict rubric that demands a baseline level of efficiency otherwise you receive no credit. This practice doesn't pair well with his very unique classroom strategy of "class is optional 90% of the time but you have to watch a lecture I recorded years ago and answer a few questions about it to learn the material and we will discuss that in class." 

TLDR: If you are not a very outgoing/proactive student (similar to me) this class will most likely be tough.

P.S. the mean for all three exams we took was ~55 with a standard deviation of ~20. He curved it based on standard deviation from the mean which means a 41 passes with a C-.
Don't do it to yourself... Just wait the semester out until a different professor can be taken.  Never seen such low averages in my life.  41% was a C- btw...

Obviously avoid this man like the plague if you can. BUT, if you're stuck taking him, understand that you will not learn anything whatsoever. There is literally zero point in expecting to actually get better at algorithms after taking 351 with Kruskal. Lectures are completely laughable. Better off not attending them tbh. TAs are... there. I mean, go say hi, little chit-chat. Not much actual help though.  You learn quickly that this class is one of the more unorthodox "sink-or-swim" experiments, if we can call it that. Btw, don't expect any semblance punctuality with grading whatsoever. Also the class is very exam-heavy. And that too only 3 exams that make up 83% of your grade. That's very stressful. And the Homeworks aren't graded that leniently either. No place for free points. And make sure you do as well as you can on the midterms because the final is just brutal. We got back our second midterm grades a week before the final. Also half the grades (as far back as grades before the first midterm took place) wasn't even graded one week before the exam. No point stressing, just chill. 

Best way to study for the exams is to
a) Understand all the past exam and practice exam material, and the homework problems and try think of different spins on them, different variations they can be asked in, etc. This helps SO much. 
b) Understand the algorithms and all topics covered on the exams extremely well conceptually. 
c) Memorize, and then change up and iterate whatever practice exam material questions provided.

If you do these things, the exams are quite frankly not extremely hard. Just not enough time to answer everything with your best attempt. Honestly, other than the final I kinda winged the exams, maybe spent a day or so studying for them. What I did not do was care to reference any of the lectures or try and learn things on the go. There are maybe 1 or 2 questions that require you to use your intuition and reason quickly. But otherwise, the exams are just practice exam and homework questions reworded or asked with some kind of twist. The optimal approach is to answer those quickly first, and then spend a lot of time to reason out the 1 or 2 thinking-heavy questions 
The exams are graded extremely harshly. Again, you'll get so many points off even when you are conceptually correct because of some little thing. You HAVE to push regrade requests after every exam. Otherwise you leave a ton of points on the table. 

You'll still probably do terrible on the exams. But so will everyone else. Our exam averages were 57, 58, and 43. So make of that what you will. 

The one saving grace of this class is the all ole mighty curve. For this Semester: A+: 85+, A: 75+, A-: 70+, B+: 65+, B: 60+, B-: 55+, C+: 51+, C: 46+, C-: 41+. 
I **thought** I did much better on the final than I actually ended up scoring. Grading, like I mentioned before, ridiculous how ridiculous it is. Just farm regrade requests if you answered a question mostly correctly but got all or most points off. One thing though was that I ended up being surprised by the relative generosity of the curve, so there's that. So for that reason and that reason alone, I'm giving this 2 stars rather than 1.

name: CMSC351
title: Algorithms
description: Prerequisite: Minimum grade of C- in CMSC250 and CMSC216. Restriction: Must be in a major within the CMNS-Computer Science department; or must be in Engineering: Computer program; or must be in the Computer Science Minor program; and Permission from the CMSC - Computer Science department. Credit only granted for: CMSC251 or CMSC351. Additional information: CMSC351 may not count as one of the required upper level CMSC courses for students who are required to have 24 upper level CMSC credits for graduation, i.e. for students who became computer science majors prior to Fall, 2002. A systematic study of the complexity of some elementary algorithms related to sorting, graphs and trees, and combinatorics. Algorithms are analyzed using mathematical techniques to solve recurrences and summations.
